ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singapore economic rice mixed plate; 'rice dish' (FOODON_03315595) used as the closest broader class. A more specific term for mixed-rice hawker dishes is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Singapore economic rice. A00LC (rice-based composite dish) is used as the closest defensible analogous code. Exact match unavailable.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific hawker dish composite. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual hawker meal configurations.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of economic rice with two vegetable dishes and one meat dish typically weighs approximately 400 g, as commonly served at Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops.

nutrient_source.title: Economic Rice (2 Vegetables, 1 Meat) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for 'Economic Rice (2 Vegetables, 1 Meat)' (cr_id F05100041). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, and several vitamins are not reported in the HPB record and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (steamed, Asian-style)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database specifically for Singapore economic rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is assigned based on the GI of steamed white rice (Asian-style), which is the dominant carbohydrate contributor in this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 59.44) / 100 ≈ 42.9, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 14.86 × 4 = 59.44 g. The two vegetable dishes and one meat dish contribute minimal additional carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 214 mg/100 g (856 mg per 400 g serving) with low dietary fibre (1.32 g/100 g) and insufficient potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy or oyster sauce to lower sodium below 200 mg/100 g, choose chicken or tofu over fatty pork, and increase vegetable portions to improve the potassium-to-sodium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (72) white rice base with 14.86 g/100 g carbohydrate and only 1.32 g/100 g dietary fibre, producing a high glycaemic load of approximately 42.9 per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller rice portion (half scoop), increase vegetable servings to raise fibre intake, choose lean braised protein over fatty cuts, and monitor post-meal blood glucose given the high glycaemic load of this dish.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (5.27 g/100 g) with unknown saturated fat fraction; sodium at 214 mg/100 g and refined white rice base are unfavourable for cardiovascular risk management
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose steamed or braised lean protein (chicken breast, tofu, or fish) over fatty pork, request less cooking oil in vegetable preparation, and limit portion size to manage sodium and total energy intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vegetable dishes provide antioxidants (vitamin C, carotenoids) with anti-inflammatory benefit, but red meat choice increases arachidonic acid (omega-6) intake which amplifies inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose chicken, fish, or tofu over red meat to minimise arachidonic acid load, and request less oil in vegetable stir-frying to reduce pro-inflammatory lipid intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economic Rice (2 Vegetables, 1 Meat), known in Mandarin as 经济饭 (Jīng Jì Fàn) and in Malay as Nasi Ekonomi, is one of Singapore's most ubiquitous and affordable hawker meals. It consists of a serving of steamed white rice accompanied by two self-selected vegetable dishes and one meat dish, chosen from a display of pre-cooked options at a hawker centre or coffee shop stall. Common vegetable choices include stir-fried kai lan, cabbage, long beans, or tofu, while meat options typically include braised pork belly, steamed chicken, fried fish, or luncheon meat. The dish is served on a single plate or tray, making it a complete one-plate meal. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 130 kcal, 5.46 g protein, 5.27 g fat, and 14.86 g carbohydrate, with 1.32 g dietary fibre and 214 mg sodium. The refined white rice base carries a high glycaemic index of approximately 72, producing a high glycaemic load per standard 400 g serving. This makes the dish a moderate-to-high concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. The dish is culturally significant as an everyday working-class meal in Singapore, reflecting the multicultural hawker food tradition. For clinical guidance: portion control of rice, selection of lean protein, and increased vegetable servings are recommended strategies to improve the nutritional profile of this meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in economic rice?
Is economic rice suitable for diabetics?
How much protein in economic rice?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100041) with a reported confidence of 0.95. HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. GI is estimated from the steamed white rice analogue in the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source; this introduces a minor estimation uncertainty but the analogue is highly defensible given white rice is the dominant carbohydrate contributor.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: detailed fatty acid fractions, fat-soluble vitamins, water-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite hawker dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05100041) as of 2026. The HPB record does not disaggregate saturated fat from total fat for this dish. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white rice (Asian-style) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 AJCN international GI tables; no direct GI measurement exists for Singapore economic rice as a composite dish. Glycaemic load per serving calculated at 400 g standard plate: GL = (72 × 59.44) / 100 ≈ 42.9. Dietary tags 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' from the input record have been removed as this dish contains meat by definition. The 'low_calorie' tag has been removed as 130 kcal/100 g (520 kcal per 400 g serving) does not qualify as low-calorie. Halal status is not confirmed as meat selection varies by stall; is_halal set to false pending stall-level verification. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03315595 used as broader class; FoodEx2 A00LC used as analogous estimate — neither is an exact match. SNOMED CT has no applicable code for this dish.
